result:
There’s a pattern I’ve noticed across very different areas of life: progress almost always feels worse from the inside than it looks from the outside.

You see it in people learning a language, in founders starting companies, in artists trying to find a voice, in students working through difficult topics. From far away, we see the “story”: a path, more or less straight, from not-knowing to knowing, from weak to strong, from nothing to something. From inside, it feels more like wandering in a fog where the landmarks keep rearranging themselves.

The strange thing is: the fog is not a bug in the process—it is the process.

Think about a time when you understood something suddenly. A concept in math that “clicked,” or a social dynamic that snapped into focus, or a skill that suddenly felt easy one day. It’s tempting to tell the story backwards: you struggled, and then you “got it.” But what actually happened in your mind during the period when you didn’t get it?

You were building hooks.

You didn’t know you were building hooks. It felt like confusion, or repetition, or maybe even failure. But those half-understandings, the moments of “I sort of see it, but…,” were laying down mental structures that later made the insight possible. Only when enough hooks exist does a new piece of information suddenly “fit” in a way that feels obvious.

From the outside, people see the moment of “obvious.” From the inside, you mostly just remember the confusion and the relief. So you develop an intuition that progress feels like relief, like clarity, like a sense of moving forward. When it doesn’t feel like that, you think you’re failing.

The truth is almost inverted: if you’re working near the edge of your ability, progress usually feels like repeated contact with confusion. Not because you are uniquely slow or incapable, but because any path into the unknown has to go through a region where your existing mental models aren’t yet useful.

The same pattern appears at larger scales. Consider how people change their views on big issues—political, philosophical, moral. It’s rare that a single argument just flips someone’s position (though that’s how we like to tell the story later). What usually happens is that over months or years, they see anomalies: small mismatches between their beliefs and their experience. They stash them away. They find temporary stories to patch over the cracks.

For a long while, this just feels like unease. They can’t quite articulate the problem; they only know that certain situations produce an uncomfortable friction between what they expect and what they see. At some point—with enough of these anomalies—some new organizing principle appears that makes them say, “Ah. That’s what’s going on.” It isn’t that the final argument was so powerful. It’s that the groundwork of dissonance was finally dense enough to support a new frame.

That groundwork felt, the entire time, like something going wrong. But it was actually the only way for something deeper to go right.

This mismatch between internal experience and external appearance has consequences. People quit things too early because they read the “fog” as evidence that they are off-track, not realizing that the fog is the terrain you must cross before you have any map at all. They underestimate others because they confuse practiced fluency (which looks like certainty) with actual understanding (which usually coexists with a sense of how much is unknown). They also overestimate others, imagining that everyone else feels clear and confident most of the time, while they alone are stumbling.

Ironically, the people doing the most interesting work are often the ones who feel least sure of what they’re doing. They see more of the complexity; they notice more of the anomalies. Confidence and accuracy don’t move in lockstep. There’s an idea called the Dunning–Kruger effect that’s often simplified to “the less people know, the more confident they are,” but the more interesting point is that when you start to learn a domain, your uncertainty grows faster than your skills. You’re suddenly aware of how much there is to know. You move from “I don’t know that I don’t know” to “I very much know that I don’t know.”

Then, if you keep going, your skill finally begins to catch up to your sense of the space. From the outside, people now see competence and mistake it for constant inner confidence. From the inside, you remember all the stages where you were lost.

The way we tell stories amplifies this distortion. Memoirs, interviews, case studies—they’re all told with hindsight. Hindsight compresses the timeline, filters out the dead ends, and imposes a logic on events that wasn’t visible at the time. This is not malicious; it’s often the only way to make a narrative comprehensible. But it gives a false impression that lives unfold as arcs rather than as clouds of attempts.

In real time, there are always more branches. You make a choice, but at the moment of choosing you usually aren’t thinking, “This is the turning point of my life, the hinge of the story.” You’re thinking something like, “I guess this seems slightly less bad than the alternatives.” Later, when telling the story, you reinterpret that moment as decisive, as though you moved with deliberate purpose. The hesitations, the nights you nearly chose differently, the half-decisions that went nowhere—those mostly get edited out.

So one quiet skill of living is learning to tolerate being inside the part of the story that doesn’t yet make sense, and not demanding that it feel like a story too early.

This applies not just to individuals but to cultures. We are, collectively, in a constant state of partial understanding about the systems we live in: technologies, economies, ecologies, institutions. We adopt something—cars, the internet, smartphones, social media, AI—long before we understand its full consequences. We stumble through the fog at a civilizational scale. Later observers will say, “That was the period when society transitioned from X to Y,” as if we knew that at the time.

Right now, for example, our relationship with algorithmic systems is still mostly at the “fog” stage. We see individual phenomena—recommendation feeds that shape attention, generative models that turn text into images or code, decision systems used in hiring or policing—but we don’t yet have stable, shared concepts for what it means to live among these systems, how to govern them, or how they reshape ideas like authorship, responsibility, and knowledge. We feel the anomalies: news that seems tuned more for engagement than for informing, social spaces that produce new forms of loneliness, productivity tools that both empower and overwhelm.

Future writers may describe this as “the early 21st-century period of algorithmic integration,” or something similarly neat. But from here, it feels messy and confusing because it is messy and confusing. We’re still building the hooks. Our debates are partial, our vocabularies inadequate. That’s exactly what you’d expect at this stage—yet to many, the confusion feels like proof that something is uniquely broken about now.

One of the most stabilizing shifts you can make, individually, is to reinterpret that feeling. Imagine if, whenever you noticed “I am confused; I don’t know what I’m doing,” you tagged that not as a red flag but as a sign that you are actually touching the frontier of your current understanding. It doesn’t mean you’re right. It doesn’t mean you’ll succeed. But it does mean you’re not just orbiting inside the region where everything is already familiar and safe.

You still want feedback; you still want to notice when you’re repeating the same mistake in a circle. Confusion plus reflection is different from confusion plus flailing. But reflection is only possible if you don’t immediately retreat from the discomfort.

This shows up in an everyday way in conversations. The most fruitful conversations usually pass through a moment where one or both people realize that their initial framings were too simple. The topic gets blurry. Definitions wobble. It’s tempting to steer back to safer ground, where everyone nods. But if you stay in the blurred region, clarifying terms and testing assumptions, you often emerge with a new shared understanding that neither person could have generated alone. Again: the fog is not a detour; it’s the route.

If this is true, then a big part of education—formal and informal—should be helping people become more comfortable in that uncomfortable zone. Not by pretending it’s pleasant, but by naming it, normalizing it, and giving people tools to navigate it: how to break problems down, how to ask better questions, how to notice when confusion is productive versus when it’s a sign that you lack a prerequisite.

We tend to equate “feeling smart” with fluency and quick answers. But outside of narrow domains, the more useful quality is the willingness to say, “I don’t know yet, but I’m willing to stay with the question long enough to find a better way of not knowing.” That willingness is fragile; it gets crushed easily by environments that punish visible uncertainty or over-reward slick confidence.

It helps to remember: everyone you admire, in any field, spent long periods not knowing what they were doing. Often, they still feel that way in the areas where they’re currently pushing themselves. You just don’t see that part, because you can’t see inside their experience, and because the stories you encounter have already been smoothed.

From the outside, progress looks like a line. From the inside, it feels like a fog. You can’t make the fog go away. But you can learn to recognize it as a sign that you’re exactly where growth happens: somewhere you can’t fully explain yet, with more questions than answers, building the invisible hooks that only become obvious in retrospect.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
